toxic-alcohols-primary.name: Patient
toxic-alcohols-primary.chiefComplaint: Person found unconscious with empty antifreeze container
toxic-alcohols-primary.variantDescription: Methanol vs ethylene glycol, osmolal gap, metabolic acidosis with elevated AG, fomepizole
keyFindings: Methanol → formic acid (optic nerve damage, blindness); Ethylene glycol → oxalic acid (renal failure, calcium oxalate crystals)
Both cause: elevated anion gap metabolic acidosis + elevated osmolal gap
Early: elevated osmolal gap (parent alcohol); Late: elevated anion gap (toxic metabolites)
Antidote: fomepizole (alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitor) — blocks conversion to toxic metabolites
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Toxic Alcohol Ingestion

mcqs.question: What is the most important consideration in this case?

options.text: Methanol → formic acid (optic nerve damage, blindness); Ethylene glycol → oxalic acid (renal failure, calcium oxalate crystals)
options.explanation: This is the key concept.
Further evaluation is warranted.

references.title: AACT Position Paper: Single-Dose Activated Charcoal

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

questions.stem: A patient presents 2 hours after ingesting an unknown quantity of acetaminophen. Serum APAP level at 4 hours post-ingestion is 220 mcg/mL. Using the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, what is the appropriate management?

options.text: Start IV N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — the level is above the treatment line
options.explanation: On the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, the treatment line at 4 hours is 150 mcg/mL (FDA line) or 200 mcg/mL (original Rumack-Matthew line). At 220 mcg/mL, the level exceeds both thresholds. IV NAC (150 mg/kg loading, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h) should be started immediately.

options.text: Recheck level at 8 hours before deciding on treatment
options.explanation: With a clearly supratherapeutic level at 4 hours, treatment should begin immediately. Delaying NAC beyond 8 hours post-ingestion significantly increases risk of hepatotoxicity.

options.text: Administer activated charcoal only — NAC is not indicated within 4 hours of ingestion
options.explanation: Activated charcoal may be beneficial if given within 1-2 hours of ingestion, but it is NOT a substitute for NAC when the level indicates toxicity. NAC should be started as soon as possible.

options.text: Obtain LFTs and treat only if transaminases are elevated
options.explanation: Hepatotoxicity from acetaminophen is delayed (ALT typically rises at 24-72 hours). Normal LFTs at 4 hours do not exclude impending liver injury. The nomogram guides treatment BEFORE liver damage occurs.

carbon-monoxide-primary.name: Patient
carbon-monoxide-primary.chiefComplaint: Family presenting with headaches during winter with gas heating
carbon-monoxide-primary.variantDescription: Cherry-red appearance myth, carboxyhemoglobin levels, pulse ox falsely normal, hyperbaric oxygen
keyFindings: CO binds hemoglobin with 250x affinity of O2 → carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) → tissue hypoxia
Pulse oximetry reads FALSELY NORMAL (cannot distinguish COHb from OxHb)
Symptoms by level: headache (10-20%), confusion (20-40%), seizures/coma (>40%), death (>60%)
Diagnosis: co-oximetry (venous or arterial) — measures COHb directly
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Carbon Monoxide Poisoning

mcqs.question: What is the most important consideration in this case?

options.text: CO binds hemoglobin with 250x affinity of O2 → carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) → tissue hypoxia
options.explanation: This is the key concept.
Further evaluation is warranted.

salicylate-primary.name: Patient
salicylate-primary.chiefComplaint: Elderly patient with confusion and tinnitus after accidental aspirin overdose
salicylate-primary.variantDescription: Mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, tinnitus, alkalinization, hemodialysis indications
keyFindings: Classic: mixed respiratory alkalosis (direct medullary stimulation) + anion gap metabolic acidosis
Early: respiratory alkalosis dominates; Late: metabolic acidosis predominates
Symptoms: tinnitus, diaphoresis, nausea, hyperthermia, altered mental status
Treatment: IV sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine (pH >7.5) — traps ionized salicylate in urine
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Salicylate Poisoning

mcqs.question: What is the most important consideration in this case?

options.text: Classic: mixed respiratory alkalosis (direct medullary stimulation) + anion gap metabolic acidosis
options.explanation: This is the key concept.
Further evaluation is warranted.
